Four files are written to `cfg$out_dir`: the enumeration table, the full measurement model with design-based confidence intervals (one row per probability, with `item`, `category`, `class`, and the applied `class_label` as columns), the class-label sheet (`K`, `Label`, `Description`; edit it and point `cfg$class_labels_csv` at it to take control of naming), and the case-level assignments for **all** respondents, including those scored outside the fit in the scoring section (posteriors, modal class, and maximum posterior), ready to merge back into the survey data.

# Adapting this script to your survey: a checklist

1.  **Write a dataset adapter.** Copy `prepare_data_lapop.R`, edit only its `pd` block (path, design-variable names, item codes and English names, nonresponse regex, wording template, demographic recodes), source it in the adapter chunk, and point `cfg$data`/`cfg$items` at its outputs. Nothing else in this document changes.
2.  **Set `cfg$items`** to your indicator columns, each coded as consecutive integers from 1 over its substantive answers. Items may have **different numbers of categories**. Put any "Don't know" or missing code in `cfg$na_codes` (or use `NA` directly); those responses are treated as missing, the model is fit on complete cases, and missing-item respondents are assigned a class in the scoring section. The category-count and missing table after Section 6 lets you confirm the coding.
3.  **Set `cfg$strata`, `cfg$psu`, `cfg$weight`** to your design columns. This is the analogue of `svyset psu [pweight = w], strata(stratum)`. If your file ships **replicate weights** instead of strata and PSU identifiers, replace each `svydesign(...)` plus `as.svrepdesign(...)` pair with a single `svrepdesign(repweights = ..., weights = ..., type = ...)`; the rest of the variance code is unchanged.
4.  **Set `cfg$aux`** to the demographics you want in the profile. Demographics used to build the weights (age, for instance) are fine and often helpful here; the only variables to exclude are the survey items that defined the classes.
5.  **Optionally set `cfg$K_force`** to pin the number of classes when the weighted BIC prefers more than are interpretable. Leave it `NULL` to select $K$ by BIC (Section 8); set it to an integer within `cfg$K_range` to override the pick. The enumeration table still prints either way, so the BIC evidence remains visible alongside the forced choice.
6.  **Run, then read enumeration first.** Choose $K$ on the weighted BIC, sanity-checked against entropy and interpretability (or pin it with `cfg$K_force`); the validation check should show the EM matching `poLCA` at unit weights before you trust the weighted output.
7.  **Mind the degrees of freedom.** Your design-based intervals carry roughly (number of PSUs minus number of strata) degrees of freedom; with few PSUs they will be wide, and that width is honest.
8.  **Read the demographic profile as design-based prevalences (the profiling section).** It reports each class's prevalence within each demographic level, and the differences between groups, with design-based intervals from the JKn replicate weights. Region is included even when it is the stratum, because a within-region prevalence is a domain estimate rather than a regression coefficient. Wide bands reflect few PSUs in a group, not an error. These estimates treat the measurement model as given and do not propagate the step-one latent class uncertainty.
9.  **Keep `survey_lca_source.R` next to this document.** It holds the plotting and data-prep helpers, the weighted EM engine, and the LLM class-labeling functions, and is sourced in the setup chunk. The functions are dataset-agnostic, so the same file serves any study, but the render fails if it is not in the working directory.
10. **Score respondents outside the fit** with `score_lca(newdata, fit_w, cfg$items)` (the scoring section): it assigns a class to anyone carrying the item columns, including a fresh data frame, as long as those items are recoded the same way.
11. **Class labels.** By default the LLM drafts a label and description per class (one call per class, frozen to `class_labels_llm.csv` in `cfg$out_dir`), and every downstream figure and table uses them. To take control: edit that file, or write your own with columns `K`, `Label`, `Description`, point `cfg$class_labels_csv` at it, and set `cfg$K_force`; the LLM then never runs. At work set `cfg$compass_base_url` and `cfg$llm_model`, and set `cfg$llm_key_env <- NULL` so the `.Rprofile`-supplied key is used; the key itself never appears in any script.

```{r session}
future::plan(future::sequential)   # close any parallel workers

```
